claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) was the second most common variant globally in Group A (symptom onset ≤12 months with cardiomyopathy) with n=27 occurrences, and was the most common variant in the Asia-Pacific region for Group A (n=26), but was not frequently reported in any other region.

citations.quote: c.1935C>A was the second most common globally and the most common in Asia‐Pacific but was not frequently reported in any other region.

claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) was reported a total of 42 times across all phenotypic subgroups (Groups A, B, and C) in the Pompe Registry dataset of 1,079 patients.

c.1935C>A is listed as a known (previously published) variant present in Group A patients in Table 5, which enumerates all variants found in classic infantile Pompe disease patients (onset ≤12 months with cardiomyopathy).
c.1935C>A was found in homozygosity in 7 CRIM-positive Group A patients (classic infantile Pompe disease with onset ≤12 months and cardiomyopathy), and also in homozygosity in Group B patients.

The homozygous variant c.1935C>A, typically associated with classic infantile Pompe disease (Group A), was also reported in Group B patients; upon review, these Group B patients appear to have had cardiomyopathy reported in the Registry after 12 months of age, suggesting misclassification.

citations.quote: homozygous substitution (missense) variants c.1843G>A, c.1933G>A, and c.1935C>A, typically found in classic infantile Pompe disease patients, were reported in both Groups A and B (Table 6). Upon careful review, the patients in Group B appear to have had cardiomyopathy reported in the Registry after 12 months of age and are expected to be in Group A.

claims.text: In Asia-Pacific Group A, c.1935C>A accounted for nearly half of reported variants and was the fourth most common variant globally; it is linked in cis with the pseudodeficiency variant c.1726G>A in Asia-Pacific patients.

citations.quote: In Asia‐Pacific, c.1935C>A was reported most often, but was not among the top five reported in other regions. Nevertheless, this variant was the fourth most common globally due to the input of patients from Asia‐Pacific where it accounted for nearly half of reported variants in Group A. Not unexpectedly, c1726G>A also was common in Group A in this region since c.1935C>A and c.1726G>A are linked in cis in patients in the Asia‐Pacific.

claims.text: Homozygosity for c.1935C>A in Group A is expected to lead to the classic infantile Pompe disease phenotype (onset ≤12 months with cardiomyopathy), consistent with the severity rating of variants found in Group A patients.

citations.quote: With regard to the severity of *GAA* variants, those identified in Group A (Table 5) are "very severe" as they were found in patients with very early onset of first symptoms (The Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO), 2019) and cardiomyopathy (HP:0001638), that is, patients with classic infantile Pompe disease. Homozygosity for any of these variants is expected to lead to this phenotype, and this expectation is largely confirmed by the data on homozygous genotypes presented in Table 6.

claims.text: Three homozygous variants including c.1935C>A were reported in both Groups A and B, while all other homozygous variants in Groups B and C were group-specific.

citations.quote: three homozygous variants (c.1843G>A, c.1933G>A, and c.1935C>A) were reported in both Groups A and B, and one (c.2481+102_2646+31del) in both Groups A and C.

claims.text: The reference sequences used for GAA variant nomenclature in this paper include NM_000152.5 for the coding region based on GRCh38 (NC_000017.10 for intronic/genomic coordinates in Supporting Tables).

citations.quote: The following reference sequences were used to specify GAA variants: NM_000152.5 for the coding region conform the Ensembl sequence of the forward strand on Chromosome 17: 80,101,556‐80,119,879 [GRCh38:CM000679.2]; protein id [NP_000143.2]
